title: Cambridge CAE 3 — Test 1 (Reading & Use of English)

sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read three extracts which are all concerned in some way with fashion. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Extract 1: Henri-Lloyd launches women’s fashion footwear

Henri-Lloyd, the UK sailing clothes brand, is launching a new women’s fashion footwear collection. Spanning eight styles in a multitude of colourways, the collection is deemed to be modern and sporty, a bit like the brand itself. According to the company’s commercial director, Graham Allen, the women’s line is very much a response to consumer and retailer demand. ‘It is a really exciting move for us and is a natural extension of our existing men’s footwear business,’ he says.

Hailed as ‘essential staples in the wardrobe of any actively minded woman’, the new collection is designed to encapsulate Henri-Lloyd’s spirit of adventure, but with a feminine twist. The collection is designed to target a certain mindset rather than a particular demographic, and pinning the Henri-Lloyd team down to a particular age group can be tricky.

The company prides itself on its pioneering styles, aiming to produce the highest quality products with strong functionality and design. Ongoing involvement with the BMW ORACLE racing team has helped cement its technical marine roots with fashion. Outside the UK, Henri-Lloyd’s biggest export markets are Italy, Sweden and the USA. The brand can be found in 2,006 stores in 23 countries worldwide.

Extract from a book on designing costumes for the theatre

Two important considerations when designing historical, or ‘period’, costumes are shape and colour. Whereas it is possible to eliminate the colour aspect by designing in black, white and grey — as in the early days of television — it is impossible to create a costume which has no shape. Costumes with bad or weak shapes are all too common and it is necessary to train the eye to select what is telling and pertinent and to incorporate these elements into the design. This chapter sketches the changing shape of period clothes; but it should not be thought that the consideration of shape applies only to historical dress — it is just as important when designing a comedy horse or a spaceman’s costume, neither of which are in any way related to the historical scene.

The drawings I have made show people from the early medieval period to the 1930s. It must be understood that I have tried to select figures which will show fairly clearly the progression of costume style. Transitional styles — often very interesting, I fully acknowledge — have necessarily had to be left out. Change of shape is affected by the social history of the period, the availability and discovery of fabrics and the human desire for change. The period covered is indeed a large one, but it must be appreciated that in the early days fashion, and therefore shape, changed very slowly; this has accelerated until today fashion changes almost yearly.

Extract: What’s happening in fashion retail?

According to UK prediction consultant Tim Harvey, ‘The emerging shopping culture is one that values quirky individuality. We want things that look more distinctive and less mass-produced, that have content and meaning specific to us.’ At present we can see large chain stores struggling to create an intimate space within their vast warehouse-like shopping emporiums and win back disgruntled customers. Even Clothes4U are developing a boutique-style environment, with a designer range sold in selected stores from a screened-off area. On the other hand, the news that computer chip technology to aid colour co-ordination — a top beeps if it doesn’t match your choice of trousers or skirt — is to be brought in by the company for their mainstream lines, is a declaration of how far off the mark they have strayed from understanding what shopping is all about. Their judgement is not always sound.

Unfortunately, a huge disparity has emerged in the levels of service. And service is where retailers can add value to the experience of shopping for clothes, because, where matters of image are concerned, buying clothes requires a certain amount of emotional involvement. More than the mere removal of garments in a cramped changing room, most of us invest the new clothes we try on with the ability to augment confidence or bolster self-esteem. Clever retailers know how potentially therapeutic any shopping trip is and can train staff to understand the anxieties we bring to the dressing room mirror.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: 1. What point is being made about the new collection of shoes?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'They fit well with the company’s established image.'. - Tại sao đúng: Trong đoạn 1, bộ sưu tập giày mới được mô tả là 'modern and sporty, a bit like the brand itself' (hiện đại và thể thao, hơi giống bản thân thương hiệu). Điều này cho thấy dòng sản phẩm giày này rất phù hợp và thống nhất với hình ảnh phong cách thể thao, hiện đại vốn có của hãng. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì văn bản nêu rõ bộ sưu tập nhắm tới 'a certain mindset rather than a particular demographic' (một tư duy nhất định hơn là nhóm nhân khẩu học cụ thể) và rất khó để định vị một nhóm tuổi cụ thể (pinning down to a particular age group can be tricky). + ❌ B sai vì đây là 'a natural extension' (một sự mở rộng tự nhiên từ mảng giày nam sẵn có) chứ không phải là sự đi lệch hay thay đổi đột phá về phong cách. + ❌ D sai vì ý tưởng xuất phát từ nhu cầu thực tế của người tiêu dùng và nhà bán lẻ ('a response to consumer and retailer demand') chứ không phải hoàn toàn phát kiến từ nội bộ công ty tự thân nghĩ ra. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 8 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. They fit well with the company’s established image.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. They are intended to appeal specifically to younger women.
B. They represent a departure in style from the company’s clothes.
C. They fit well with the company’s established image.
D. The initial idea for them came from within the company.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 2. What idea is repeated in the text?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'the image of the shoes as practical and stylish.'. - Tại sao đúng: Sự đan xen giữa tính thời trang/phong cách (stylish/modern) và tính thực tế/tiện dụng (practical/functionality) được lặp lại xuyên suốt văn bản: + Đoạn 1: 'modern and sporty' (hiện đại và thể thao). + Đoạn 2: 'essential staples in the wardrobe of any actively minded woman... spirit of adventure, but with a feminine twist' (những món đồ thiết yếu cho phụ nữ năng động... tinh thần phiêu lưu kết hợp sự nữ tính). + Đoạn 3: 'highest quality products with strong functionality and design' (sản phẩm chất lượng cao nhất với tính năng và thiết kế mạnh mẽ) và 'technical marine roots with fashion' (gốc rễ hàng hải kỹ thuật kết hợp với thời trang). - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì độ phủ sóng rộng rãi chỉ đề cập duy nhất một lần ở câu cuối ('found in 2,006 stores in 23 countries'). + ❌ B sai vì việc hợp tác kỹ thuật với đội đua thuyền BMW ORACLE chỉ được nhắc tới một lần ở đoạn 3. + ❌ D sai vì dòng giày nam chỉ được đề cập qua để giới thiệu nguồn gốc dòng giày nữ ở đoạn 1, không nhấn mạnh tính chất hướng tới tương lai. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 8 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. the image of the shoes as practical and stylish. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. the widespread availability of the shoes
B. the company’s clever use of technical associations
C. the image of the shoes as practical and stylish
D. the forward-looking nature of the men’s footwear line
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 3. What point is the writer making about costume design?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'Shape is a potentially more problematic issue than colour.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả lập luận: 'Whereas it is possible to eliminate the colour aspect by designing in black, white and grey... it is impossible to create a costume which has no shape.' (Trong khi có thể loại bỏ khía cạnh màu sắc bằng cách thiết kế màu đen, trắng và xám... thì không thể tạo ra một trang phục không có hình dáng). Ngoài ra tác giả còn bổ sung 'Costumes with bad or weak shapes are all too common' (Trang phục có phom dáng xấu hoặc yếu là quá phổ biến). Điều này chứng minh hình dáng là vấn đề khó khăn và phức tạp hơn màu sắc rất nhiều trong thiết kế trang phục sân khấu. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì bài viết chỉ ra lỗi phom dáng yếu rất phổ biến nói chung, chứ không nói các nhà thiết kế giàu kinh nghiệm vẫn thường mắc lỗi này. + ❌ B sai vì tác giả chỉ lấy ví dụ tivi thời kỳ đầu đen trắng để minh họa tính khả thi của việc lược bỏ màu sắc, không khuyên các nhà thiết kế sân khấu tránh dùng màu sáng. + ❌ C sai vì chương sách này viết riêng về thiết kế trang phục cho sân khấu kịch ('designing costumes for the theatre'), không đề cập đến thiết kế phi sân khấu. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 9 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. Shape is a potentially more problematic issue than colour.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. Even experienced designers can make mistakes with the shape of a costume.
B. Stage costume designers should beware of using bright colours.
C. The advice given is also relevant to non-theatrical designers.
D. Shape is a potentially more problematic issue than colour.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 4. When she uses the phrase ‘often very interesting, I fully acknowledge’ (lines 79–80 / in paragraph 2), the writer is
questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'anticipating a possible criticism from readers.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả viết: 'Transitional styles — often very interesting, I fully acknowledge — have necessarily had to be left out.' (Các phong cách chuyển giao — tôi hoàn toàn thừa nhận rằng chúng thường rất thú vị — đã buộc phải bị loại bỏ). Việc thêm cụm từ thừa nhận này vào giữa câu cho thấy tác giả đang dự đoán trước lời chỉ trích của độc giả hoặc giới chuyên môn về việc thiếu sót các phong cách chuyển tiếp thú vị trong các bản vẽ của mình, và giải thích lý do tại sao chúng phải bị lược bỏ (do khuôn khổ cuốn sách cần tập trung vào tiến trình rõ ràng). - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ B sai vì tác giả không bổ sung những phần thiếu này vào sách, mà chỉ giải thích tại sao bỏ qua. + ❌ C sai vì tác giả không thừa nhận phân tích của mình có lỗi hay không hoàn hảo, chỉ thừa nhận việc chọn lọc là bắt buộc. + ❌ D sai vì không liên quan đến quan điểm truyền thống hay hiện đại. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 9 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. anticipating a possible criticism from readers.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. anticipating a possible criticism from readers.
C. admitting that her analysis is necessarily imperfect.
D. accepting that her views are rather traditional.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 5. What does the writer say about Clothes4U?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'Their judgement is not always sound.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả nhận xét việc Clothes4U áp dụng công nghệ chip máy tính hỗ trợ phối màu (kêu bíp nếu áo không hợp với quần/váy) cho dòng sản phẩm chủ lưu của họ là: '...is a declaration of how far off the mark they have strayed from understanding what shopping is all about.' (là một lời tuyên bố về việc họ đã đi lệch hướng xa như thế nào khỏi việc hiểu mua sắm thực sự là gì). Câu tiếp theo củng cố nhận định này: 'Their judgement is not always sound' (Quyết định/đánh giá của họ không phải lúc nào cũng sáng suốt). - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì công nghệ này bị tác giả chê là đi chệch hướng, không phải đang thu hút thêm khách hàng. + ❌ C sai vì Clothes4U có phản ứng trước sự thay đổi văn hóa mua sắm bằng cách mở khu boutique riêng biệt ('boutique-style environment'), chỉ là họ có quyết định sai lầm khác về mặt công nghệ. + ❌ D sai vì việc họ thử nghiệm dòng sản phẩm thiết kế riêng ở góc boutique không bị coi là thiếu khôn ngoan, mà chính quyết định áp dụng công nghệ chip bíp mới bị chỉ trích. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 9 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. Their judgement is not always sound.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. Their use of new technology is attracting more customers.
B. Their judgement is not always sound.
C. They have failed to address a change in the world of fashion.
D. They are unwisely moving to specialist markets.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 6. The writer implies that successful clothes shops encourage their staff to
questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'exploit their customers’ insecurities.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả viết: 'Clever retailers know how potentially therapeutic any shopping trip is and can train staff to understand the anxieties we bring to the dressing room mirror.' (Các nhà bán lẻ thông minh biết rằng bất kỳ chuyến mua sắm nào cũng có khả năng trị liệu tâm lý và có thể đào tạo nhân viên hiểu được những mối lo âu chúng ta mang đến trước gương phòng thay đồ). Từ 'anxieties' (những lo âu về ngoại hình, vóc dáng) chính là 'insecurities' (sự tự ti, thiếu tự tin). Bằng cách thấu hiểu và xoa dịu những lo âu này để thuyết phục khách hàng mua đồ, các cửa hàng đang 'khai thác/tận dụng các điểm tự ti của khách hàng' để tăng doanh số. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ B, C, D không được đề cập hay ngụ ý trong phần viết về vai trò của nhân viên cửa hàng. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 9 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. exploit their customers’ insecurities.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: B. allow customers room to make their own choices.
C. show an interest in the lives of their customers.
D. find a point of similarity between themselves and their customers.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a magazine article about boat design. Six paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A–G the one which fits each gap (7–12). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use.
sections.passageHtml:
The Boat of My Dreams

Tom Cunliffe describes the process of building the yacht of his dreams.

If you are looking at the photo of the boat under sail, you might think that her owner must be some kind of lost-soul romantic. Perhaps I am, though I doubt it.

[ Gap 7 ]

Constructed in timber treated with a penetrating glue, she is totally impervious to water. Thus she has all the benefits of a glass fibre boat yet looks like, feels like and sails like the real thing.

Although Nigel Irens, the designer, and Ed Burnett, his right-hand man, are adept with computer-assisted design programs, Irens initially drew this boat on a paper napkin, and only later transferred his ideas to the computer. After this had generated a set of lines, he carved a model, just as boatyards did in the days of sail. Together we considered the primary embryonic vessel, then fed the design back into the electronic box for modification.

[ Gap 8 ]

Her appearance is ageless, her motion at sea is a pleasure and her accommodation, much of it in reclaimed pitch pine, emanates an atmosphere of deep peace. Maybe this is because she was drawn purely as a sailing craft, without reference to any furniture we might put into her. That is the well-tried method of the sea.

[ Gap 9 ]

Her sails were heavy, and she had no pumped water, no electricity and no electronics. We had a bucket for a toilet and we cooked on a paraffin stove. Yet she was the kindest, easiest boat that I have ever sailed at sea.

[ Gap 10 ]

It has to be said, however, that despite being an indispensable tool in current design methods and boat-building practice, sophisticated technology frequently insulates crews from the harsh realities of maritime life. These are often the very realities they hoped to rediscover by going to sea in the first place.

[ Gap 11 ]

The occasional battle with flapping canvas is surely part of a seaman's life. And for what purpose should we abandon common sense and move our steering positions from the security of the aft end to some vulnerable perch halfway to the bow? The sad answer is that this creates a cabin like that of an ocean liner, with space for a bed larger than the one at home.

[ Gap 12 ]

Paragraphs options (A–G):

A. It’s not that I’m suggesting that sailors should go back to enduring every hardship. It’s always been important to me that my boats have a coal stove for warmth and dryness and cosy berths for sleeping. But why go cruising at all if every sail sets and furls itself?

B. Back on land, however, it is a sad fact that the very antiquity of classic boats means that they need a lot of looking after. When I had a bad injury to my back, I realised that my 15-year love affair with her had to end. Searching for a younger replacement produced no credible contenders, so I decided to build a new boat from scratch.

C. In her timeless serenity, she is the living proof that it works; that there is no need to follow current fashions to find satisfaction, and that sometimes it pays to listen to the lessons of history.

D. The next version was nearly right and by the time the final one appeared, the form was perfect. The completed boat has now crossed the North Atlantic and has won four out of her first six racing starts.

E. At the same time, having lived aboard an ancient wooden beauty in the early seventies, it’s easier to understand more of this area of the mechanics. My designer, for example, knows more about the ways of a boat on the sea than anyone I can think of.

F. This boat has benefited from all the magic of old-fashioned boat design, but it would have been a much harder job without the advances of modern know-how.

G. For me a boat should always be a boat and not a cottage on the water. When I bought an earlier boat, Hirta, in which I circumnavigated Britain for a TV race series, the previous owner observed that she had every comfort, but no luxury. During my long relationship with her, Hirta taught me how wise he was.

questionGroups.instruction: Select the correct paragraph (A–G) for each gap.

questions.explanation: Chọn F — 'This boat has benefited from all the magic...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn F mở đầu bằng: 'Perhaps I am, though I doubt it. This boat has benefited from all the magic of old-fashioned boat design, but it would have been a much harder job without the advances of modern know-how.' Câu này là sự tiếp nối hoàn hảo và trực tiếp cho câu hỏi lửng trước khoảng trống: 'Perhaps I am, though I doubt it.' (Có lẽ tôi là một kẻ lãng mạn hoài cổ, mặc dù tôi nghi ngờ điều đó). Đồng thời, thông tin ở cuối đoạn F chuẩn bị cho đoạn tiếp theo nói về kỹ thuật gỗ-epoxy 'Constructed in timber treated with a penetrating glue...'. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 10 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: F. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'The next version was nearly right...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn văn trước Gap 8 mô tả quá trình thiết kế: nhà thiết kế vẽ phác thảo trên khăn ăn, sau đó đưa vào máy tính, khắc mô hình gỗ rồi 'fed the design back into the electronic box for modification' (đưa thiết kế trở lại hộp điện tử để sửa đổi). Đoạn D tiếp nối quá trình sửa đổi này: 'The next version was nearly right and by the time the final one appeared, the form was perfect.' (Phiên bản tiếp theo gần như chính xác và khi phiên bản cuối cùng xuất hiện, phom dáng đã hoàn hảo). Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 10 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn G — 'For me a boat should always be a boat...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn văn trước Gap 9 thảo luận về phương pháp thiết kế truyền thống: vẽ chiếc thuyền thuần túy là phương tiện đi biển, không quan tâm đến đồ đạc sẽ đặt vào trong ('without reference to any furniture we might put into her. That is the well-tried method of the sea.'). Đoạn G tiếp nối tư duy này: 'For me a boat should always be a boat and not a cottage on the water.' (Đối với tôi, một chiếc thuyền luôn phải là một chiếc thuyền chứ không phải một ngôi nhà nhỏ trên mặt nước). Đồng thời, cuối đoạn G giới thiệu chiếc thuyền cũ của tác giả mang tên Hirta: 'During my long relationship with her, Hirta taught me how wise he was.' Đoạn tiếp theo bắt đầu bằng 'Her sails were heavy...' ('Her' ở đây chính là Hirta). Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 10 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: G. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'Back on land, however...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn văn trước Gap 10 mô tả trải nghiệm tuyệt vời khi đi thuyền trên biển: 'Yet she was the kindest, easiest boat that I have ever sailed at sea.' Đoạn B tạo ra sự tương phản khi trở lại đất liền: 'Back on land, however, it is a sad fact that the very antiquity of classic boats means that they need a lot of looking after.' (Tuy nhiên, khi trở lại đất liền, có một thực tế đáng buồn là tuổi đời quá cổ kính của những chiếc thuyền cổ điển đồng nghĩa với việc chúng cần rất nhiều sự chăm sóc). Điều này dẫn tới quyết định chế tạo chiếc thuyền mới từ đầu của tác giả. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 11 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'It’s not that I’m suggesting...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn văn trước Gap 11 lập luận rằng công nghệ hiện đại cô lập thủy thủ khỏi những thực tế khắc nghiệt của đời sống hàng hải ('insulates crews from the harsh realities of maritime life. These are often the very realities they hoped to rediscover...'). Đoạn A giải thích rõ hơn để tránh hiểu lầm: 'It’s not that I’m suggesting that sailors should go back to enduring every hardship.' (Không phải là tôi đang đề xuất rằng các thủy thủ nên quay lại chịu đựng mọi gian khổ). Đoạn văn sau Gap 11 tiếp nối bằng việc ủng hộ những thử thách thực tế: 'The occasional battle with flapping canvas is surely part of a seaman’s life.' Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 11 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'In her timeless serenity...'. - Tại sao đúng: Đoạn văn trước Gap 12 chỉ trích xu hướng thiết kế du thuyền bằng sợi thủy tinh hiện đại chỉ chú trọng tạo ra cabin giống như tàu biển lớn sang trọng, xa rời thực tế ('creates a cabin like that of an ocean liner...'). Đoạn C đóng vai trò kết luận bài viết bằng cách khẳng định giá trị của chiếc thuyền mới (The Westerman): 'In her timeless serenity, she is the living proof that it works; that there is no need to follow current fashions to find satisfaction, and that sometimes it pays to listen to the lessons of history.' (Trong sự thanh bình vượt thời gian của mình, cô ấy là bằng chứng sống động cho thấy nó hiệu quả; rằng không cần phải chạy theo những mốt thời thượng hiện tại để tìm thấy sự hài lòng). Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 11 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. Answer-key option. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read a magazine article about a writer. For questions 13–19, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Creative Hurdles

Jane Smiley describes her experiences as a writer.

In 2001, the year I turned 52, I started work on Good Faith, my thirteenth novel. But when I sat down at the computer to write, my heart would sink. There were about 125 pages to go, and I felt like I had wandered into a dark wood. I was afraid. Physical fears were all too familiar for me — I had been wrestling with them my whole life, but when I sat down at my computer and read what I had written the day before, I felt something different — a recoiling, an unexpected aversion.

Oh, this again. This insoluble, unjoyous labour of mine. What's the next sentence, even the next word? I didn’t know, and if I tried something I suspected it would just carry me farther down the wrong path, would be a waste of time or, worse, prolong an already prolonged piece of fraudulence.

I came up with all sorts of diagnoses for my condition. The state of the world was tempting but I refused to be convinced. The problem with the novel was not outside myself, or even in my link to human consciousness. Perhaps, I thought, it was my own professional history. Between 1977 and 1993, I had lived what was essentially a domestic life — husband, house, and children, plus university teaching. I hummed along, apparently performing my professional and family duties, but really half absent. Always my mind was elsewhere, pondering whatever novel I was writing. Only at night did I exert myself to stop thinking about my ideas for the novel, because if I allowed them in, I wouldn’t be able to sleep.

As a teenager I had been obsessed with horses. In 1993, they became a large part of my life once again — to the extent that they displaced my novels. My preoccupation went through several stages, including those of feverish research and cultivating equestrians, trainers and vets. Writing novels was now something I did when I was sitting at my desk, but not when I was cooking dinner or driving the children to school. Then I had a riding accident and broke my leg, so there was nothing really to do other than write. And then came Horse Heaven, which was, for me, book heaven. I had successfully combined my two obsessions, and the result was pure joy. As far as I was concerned the book had only one flaw — that its composition ended so soon.

But had the literary ruminations the horses had displaced been essential to novel writing? The answer to this depended on one’s theory of creativity. I hadn’t ever had much of a theory of creativity beyond making a cup of tea and sitting down at the typewriter or computer. The first and last rules were, get on with it. But perhaps that getting on with it that I had taken for granted for so many years was dependent upon those half-attentive ruminations during breadmaking and driving down the road? Or maybe teaching had stimulated me? Week after week for 14 years I had expounded about writing, given tips, analysed student stories, come up with suggestions, fielded questions. Subconsciously, I had worked out solutions for my own writing from rules I blithely laid down in class. I wasn’t doing that any more, either. Nor was I reading much fiction. In addition to not thinking much any more about my own novels, I didn’t think much any more about anyone else’s novels.

It was time to face my real fear — that my book wasn't much good. I had confidently stated more than a few times that the execution of a good novel was inherent in the idea from the first. But that was when I was certain all my ideas were good. It may come as a surprise to those who don’t care for my work that I'd hardly ever doubted the significance of any idea I’d had, and I'd had very few ideas. I’d written 12 finished works. I’d had 14 ideas. The structure of all of my completed novels was fairly apparent to me from the beginning, and I had written with an increasing energy and sense of direction as I went through the rough draft.

At the halfway mark, I stopped and read through what I had written of Good Faith. It was more interesting than I had thought. The energy of that realisation pushed me forward another 60 pages. By now, though, I was looking for terminal symptoms. One day I waited for inspiration, got some, went off in a completely new direction, then had second thoughts the next day and tried something new. This was a symptom, indeed, a symptom that I didn’t know what in the world I was doing or where I was going, and it was way too late in the game for that. My heart sank. No, my flesh turned to ice. No, my stomach churned. No, all I did was close the file on my computer, and walk away. But that was very bad. I decided to read a hundred novels.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: 13. In the first paragraph, what was the writer’s reaction to writing her thirteenth novel?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'She was surprised by her feelings about writing the book.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả viết rằng cô đã quen thuộc với những nỗi sợ hãi về thể xác trong suốt cuộc đời, nhưng khi đọc những gì mình viết hôm trước, cô cảm nhận được điều gì đó hoàn toàn khác biệt: 'I felt something different — a recoiling, an unexpected aversion.' (tôi cảm nhận một điều khác biệt — một sự lùi lại, một ác cảm bất ngờ). Từ 'unexpected' (không mong đợi, bất ngờ) chứng tỏ cô ngạc nhiên trước cảm xúc chán ghét việc viết lách đột ngột này. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì cô chỉ đề cập còn khoảng 125 trang nữa là xong chứ không hề lo lắng cuốn sách sẽ quá dài. + ❌ C sai vì cô lo sợ về sự 'vô ích, giả dối' của tác phẩm ('fraudulence') hơn là việc lo lắng về phản ứng của người đọc. + ❌ D sai vì bài viết không nhắc tới bất kỳ thời hạn hoàn thành (deadline) nào. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. She was surprised by her feelings about writing the book.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. She was concerned that the book would be overlong.
B. She was surprised by her feelings about writing the book.
C. She was worried that the book would not appeal to readers.
D. She thought the deadline set for the book was unachievable.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 14. It becomes clear in the second paragraph (actually the third paragraph in the text, labeled as the second paragraph of discussion) that the writer
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'was uncertain as to what had led to her situation.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả viết: 'I came up with all sorts of diagnoses for my condition. ... Perhaps, I thought, it was my own professional history.' (Tôi đưa ra đủ loại chẩn đoán cho tình trạng của mình... Có lẽ, tôi nghĩ, đó là lịch sử nghề nghiệp của tôi). Việc cô đưa ra nhiều 'diagnoses' khác nhau kết hợp từ phỏng đoán 'perhaps' cho thấy cô hoàn toàn không chắc chắn nguyên nhân thực sự gây ra tình trạng bế tắc của mình. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì cô không dễ bị phân tâm (ngược lại trước đây cô tập trung đến nỗi luôn 'half absent' khỏi đời sống gia đình). + ❌ B sai vì cô đang tìm cách chẩn đoán và vượt qua nó, chứ không có ý định từ bỏ viết lách hoàn toàn. + ❌ C sai vì cô không gặp rắc rối với trí nhớ hay việc nhớ lại những gì mình đã viết. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. was uncertain as to what had led to her situation.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: B. was tempted to give up writing.
C. was struggling to remember what she had written.
D. was uncertain as to what had led to her situation.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 15. What does the writer say about her book Horse Heaven?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'She had wanted to continue working on the book for longer.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả khẳng định cuốn Horse Heaven đối với cô là thiên đường ('book heaven'), kết hợp hai niềm đam mê lớn và là niềm vui thuần khiết. Cô viết: 'As far as I was concerned the book had only one flaw — that its composition ended so soon.' (Đối với tôi, cuốn sách chỉ có một khuyết điểm duy nhất — đó là việc sáng tác nó kết thúc quá sớm). Điều này đồng nghĩa với việc cô ước gì mình được tiếp tục viết nó lâu hơn nữa. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì cô nghiên cứu về ngựa vì đam mê cá nhân chứ việc nghiên cứu không phải là bắt buộc cho cuốn sách. + ❌ B sai vì khi viết cuốn này, cô chỉ viết khi ngồi vào bàn làm việc, chứ không phải nảy ra ý tưởng suốt ngày lúc nấu ăn hay đưa đón con như trước. + ❌ C sai vì cô tiếp xúc với nhiều người trong ngành ngựa nhưng bài không nói những người này xuất hiện trực tiếp trong cuốn sách. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. She had wanted to continue working on the book for longer.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. It had been necessary to do a lot of research for the book.
B. She had got ideas for the book throughout the day.
C. A lot of the people she had met featured in the book.
D. She had wanted to continue working on the book for longer.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 16. What is the purpose of the writer’s questions in the fourth paragraph (labeled as the fifth paragraph in the text)?
questions.explanation: Chọn A — 'to try and clarify what enabled her to write.'. - Tại sao đúng: Trong đoạn 5, tác giả tự hỏi: 'But had the literary ruminations the horses had displaced been essential to novel writing?... But perhaps that getting on with it... was dependent upon those half-attentive ruminations... Or maybe teaching had stimulated me?' Những câu hỏi này nhằm mục đích phân tích sâu để tìm hiểu xem yếu tố nào (những suy nghĩ vẩn vơ khi làm việc nhà, hay việc giảng dạy) thực sự là động lực âm thầm giúp cô viết lách trôi chảy suốt nhiều năm qua. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ B sai vì cô tự hỏi về cơ chế sáng tạo văn học, chứ không phải để tìm hiểu tại sao mình thích ngựa. + ❌ C sai vì cô thừa nhận mình không có lý thuyết sáng tạo lớn lao nào cả ('I hadn't ever had much of a theory of creativity'). + ❌ D sai vì các thói quen cũ là nguồn kích thích sáng tạo, cô không hề mô tả chúng gây căng thẳng cho mình. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. to try and clarify what enabled her to write. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. to try and clarify what enabled her to write
B. to understand her preoccupation with horses
C. to convince others of her theory of creativity
D. to establish how her routine had been stressful
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 17. What point does the writer illustrate in her reference to ‘rules I blithely laid down’ in paragraph 5?
questions.explanation: Chọn D — 'She had not appreciated how she benefited from such things.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả chia sẻ: 'Subconsciously, I had worked out solutions for my own writing from rules I blithely laid down in class. I wasn’t doing that any more, either.' (Một cách vô thức, tôi đã tự tìm ra giải pháp cho bài viết của mình từ những quy tắc mà tôi thản nhiên đưa ra trong lớp). Từ 'blithely' (thản nhiên, vô tư) cho thấy lúc dạy học cô không hề nhận thức hay đánh giá cao việc những lời khuyên, quy tắc giảng dạy của mình lại mang lại lợi ích to lớn cho việc viết lách của bản thân cô. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì cô không hề nói mình quá khắt khe với bài của sinh viên. + ❌ B sai vì việc đọc sách không liên quan trực tiếp đến các quy tắc cô giảng dạy trên lớp. + ❌ C sai vì phương pháp dạy học của cô thực tế rất có ích cho bản thân cô, không có lỗi sai nào ở đây. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. She had not appreciated how she benefited from such things.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. She had been too critical of her students' work.
B. She had not read enough books during this period.
C. She had been mistaken in her approach to teaching.
D. She had not appreciated how she benefited from such things.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 18. How does the writer defend her first twelve books against potential criticism?
questions.explanation: Chọn C — 'They were written with complete confidence.'. - Tại sao đúng: Tác giả tự tin khẳng định: 'I’d hardly ever doubted the significance of any idea I’d had... The structure of all of my completed novels was fairly apparent to me from the beginning, and I had written with an increasing energy and sense of direction...' (Tôi hầu như chưa bao giờ nghi ngờ ý nghĩa của bất kỳ ý tưởng nào tôi từng có... Cấu trúc của mọi cuốn tiểu thuyết đã hoàn thành đều khá rõ ràng với tôi từ đầu). Sự chắc chắn và năng lượng này bảo vệ 12 tác phẩm trước của cô trước các ý kiến trái chiều bằng việc chứng minh chúng được tạo ra bằng sự tự tin hoàn hảo. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì tác giả không đề cập đến kỹ thuật viết (technical writing) tốt hay xấu. + ❌ B sai vì cô không bàn luận về việc độc giả đồng cảm hay kết nối với sách. + ❌ D sai vì cô thừa nhận mình có rất ít ý tưởng ('I'd had very few ideas'), không nhấn mạnh tính độc đáo của ý tưởng. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 12 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. They were written with complete confidence.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. They were technically well written.
B. Many people could relate to the books.
C. They were written with complete confidence.
D. The ideas behind the books were very original.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 19. What does the writer say in the final paragraph about her attempts to finish the book?
questions.explanation: Chọn B — 'Changing her mind at this stage was unhelpful.'. - Tại sao đúng: Ở đoạn cuối, tác giả viết về việc cô liên tục thay đổi hướng đi khi viết Good Faith: 'went off in a completely new direction, then had second thoughts the next day and tried something new. This was a symptom... that I didn’t know what in the world I was doing... and it was way too late in the game for that.' (đi theo một hướng hoàn toàn mới, rồi ngày hôm sau lại suy nghĩ lại và thử một cái gì đó mới. Đây thực sự là một triệu chứng... và đã quá muộn để làm điều đó). Việc thay đổi ý định liên tục ở giai đoạn muộn này được cô xác định là triệu chứng bế tắc và hoàn toàn vô ích. - Phân tích bẫy (Traps): + ❌ A sai vì cô chỉ cảm thấy lo sợ tột cùng (flesh turned to ice, stomach churned) chứ không phải bị bệnh về thể chất. + ❌ C sai vì cô không nói phải viết lại nửa cuốn sách đã hoàn thành. + ❌ D sai vì vấn đề không nằm ở thời gian viết mà nằm ở việc cô bị mất phương hướng. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 13 Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. Changing her mind at this stage was unhelpful.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. She was feeling too unwell to continue.
B. Changing her mind at this stage was unhelpful.
C. The half she had already done needed to be rewritten first.
D. She had to increase the amount of time she spent writing.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 3, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an article about clock radios. For questions 20–34, choose from the sections (A–E). The sections may be chosen more than once.
sections.passageHtml:
SOUND THE ALARM

Stuart Harris reports

Many of us listen to the radio when we get up in the morning and most of us also require some external means to persuade us to get out of bed. Thus we have the clock radio. But how do you pick a good one? Our panel, which consisted of myself plus the inventor Tom Granger and the broadcaster Paul Bridges, tested five currently available.

A

The ‘dual alarm function’ that is advertised with this model does not allow you, as I first supposed, to be woken by the buzzer, snooze a while and then finally be driven out of bed. The instruction booklet advises you to use this function to set two different wake-up times, one for work days and one for weekends, but whose life is programmed to this extent?

Since this model costs more or less the same as the second model tested, the inclusion of a cassette player is quite a bargain — you can fall asleep to your own soothing tapes and wake up to a day without news. We all thought the quality of the radio excellent, too — if only the whole thing was smaller. It’s as big as a rugby ball. Paul Bridges said, ‘Any clock radio I buy has to leave enough space on the bedside table for my keys, wallet, glasses and telephone. Anyway, I’m completely paranoid and always book a wake-up call in case the alarm doesn’t go off.’

B

This model was voted best in the beauty stakes and overall winner. Paul Bridges declared himself ‘in love with it’, although the clock on the one he tested ‘kept getting stuck at 16.00’. I was fascinated by the digital display, with its classy grey numbers on a gentle green background. The wide snooze bar means you can tap it on the edge with your eyes shut. Unfortunately, the smooth undulations and tactile buttons, like pebbles on the beach, encouraged me to run my fingers over them as if they were keys on a piano, which proved my undoing when I finally looked at the 80-page instruction booklet.

The clock has a self-power back-up so you don’t have to reset it if someone unceremoniously pulls the plug out in order to use a hairdryer or the vacuum cleaner; this met with unanimous approval. However, we all found it a technical feat to set up - though completing the learning curve made us feel ‘cool’ and sophisticated.

C

Tom Granger described this model with its extra built-in lamp as ‘unbelievably tacky’ in the way it's made. ‘You have to wrench the funny light out of its socket to get it to work, which makes me wonder about the quality of the rest of it.’ He complained that he had to read the instruction booklet twice before he could get it to work; the clock kept leaping from 12.00 to 02.00 so he had to go round again.

The light was certainly hard to position; you would never be able to read by it - it only shines on the clock, which is illuminated anyway. Paul Bridges said he was ‘very tickled’ by the lamp idea but agreed that the radio was hard to tune. The buzzer is reminiscent of ‘action stations’ on a submarine and made me feel like hurling the whole thing across the bedroom. Interestingly, however, this model is the third most popular on the market.

D

Clearly aimed at young people, with its brightly coloured casing and matching bootlace strap, this one appealed to the child in Tom Granger and me. ‘I would choose this one because it doesn’t disappear into the background like the others,’ he said. In fact, the traditional design of the controls made it the only one we managed to set up without reading the instruction booklet. Too bad the alarm is allowed a hilarious 20-minute margin for error; the manual notes, ‘the alarm may sound about 10 minutes earlier or later than the pre-set time’. Paul Bridges scoffed at such a notion, adding that this model was ‘terribly fiddly’ and, indeed, ‘completely useless’.

E

The simplest and cheapest of all the models tested, this scored points with Tom Granger because it ‘seemed very standard and took up little space’, but also because it has old-fashioned dial tuning. ‘It's more intuitive to set up. With modern push-button tuning you're never really sure if you’ve pressed all the buttons in the right order so you can’t have confidence that the thing will actually work.’ He accepted, however, that manufacturers had been obliged to improve the quality of radios because of the advent of button-tuning. I thought the tuning rather crude, as did Paul Bridges, but we agreed that the radio quality was fine. The buzzer on this model certainly works; it succeeded in getting me out of bed in just two beeps!

sections.title: Part 5: Multiple-Choice Cloze
sections.instructions: For questions 1–12, read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
sections.passageHtml:
The early railway in Britain

In 1830, there were under 100 miles of public railway in Britain. Yet within 20 years, this (0) quantity had grown to more than 5,000 miles. By the end of the century, almost enough rail track to (1) ..... the world covered this small island, (2) ..... the nature of travel for ever and contributing to the industrial revolution that changed the (3) ..... of history in many parts of the world.

Wherever railways were introduced, economic and social progress quickly (4) ..... . In a single day, rail passengers could travel hundreds of miles, (5) ..... previous journey times by huge margins and bringing rapid travel within the (6) ..... ordinary people. Previously, many people had never ventured beyond the outskirts of their towns and villages. The railway brought them (7) ..... freedom and enlightenment.

In the 19th century, the railway in Britain (8) ..... something more than just the business of carrying goods and passengers. Trains were associated with romance, adventure and, frequently, (9) ..... luxury. But the railways (10) ..... more than revolutionise travel; they also (11) ..... a distinctive and permanent mark on the British landscape. Whole towns and industrial centres (12) ..... up around major rail junctions, monumental bridges and viaducts crossed rivers and valleys and the railway stations themselves became desirable places to spend time between journeys.

Example:

0      A. amount      B. figure      C. sum      D. quantity      (Correct: D)

sections.instructions: For questions 13–27, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0). Write your answers in CAPITAL LETTERS.
sections.passageHtml:
Enjoy the benefits of stress!

Are you looking forward to another busy week? You should be according (0) to some experts. They argue that the stress encountered in (13) ..... daily lives is not only good for us, but essential to survival. They say that the response to stress, which creates a chemical called adrenalin, helps the mind and body to act quickly (14) ..... emergencies. Animals and human beings use it to meet the hostile conditions (15) ..... exist on the planet.

The pressures of everyday life are undeniable, but many experts consider the current strategies we use to deal with them to (16) ..... inadequate and often dangerous. They believe that (17) ..... of trying to manage our response to stress by (18) ..... of drugs or relaxation techniques, we must actually exploit stress. Apparently, research shows that people (19) ..... create conditions of stress for (20) ..... by doing exciting and risky sports or looking for challenges, cope much better with life’s problems. Activities of this type (21) ..... been shown to create a lot of emotion; people may actually cry or feel extremely uncomfortable. But there is a point (22) ..... which they realise they have succeeded and know that it was a positive experience. This is because we learn through challenge and difficulty. That’s (23) ..... we get our wisdom. Few of (24) ..... , unfortunately, understand (25) ..... fact. For example, many people believe they suffer from stress at work, and take time off (26) ..... a result. Yet it has been found in some companies that by far (27) ..... healthiest people are those with the most responsibility.

So next time you're in a stressful situation, just remember that it will be a positive learning experience and could also benefit your health!

sections.passageHtml:
Autumn colours

A new term is rapidly gaining (0) recognition in the American language - a ‘leaf peeper’ is someone who, in autumn, is on the (28) ..... for areas where the leaves of deciduous trees have changed colour. In New England, in the USA, ‘leaf peeping’ is big business, generating millions of dollars annually.

The first report that leaves are changing colour sets off an (29) ..... of ‘peepers’ thus causing serious obstruction on some roads. Thousands of people log on to websites in their (30) ..... to find the location of the trees that have foliage in the most (31) ..... colours.

The popularity of ‘leaf tourism’ is well established in New England, and the changing colours of autumn provide an annual (32) ..... point. The colours vary from year to year since the (33) ..... of the colour is (34) ..... on the chemical composition of the dying leaves. After a long dry summer, leaves often turn bright red, while cloudy autumn days will produce less spectacular yellows. If climate change leads to (35) ..... hot, dry summers in the northern hemisphere, then countries in Europe can expect summers not (36) ..... those across the Atlantic. They will then enjoy a kaleidoscope of (37) ..... colour to rival the deep reds and blazing oranges seen in New England.

sections.title: Part 8: Gapped Sentences
sections.instructions: For questions 38–42, think of one word only which can be used appropriately in all three sentences. Write the word in CAPITAL LETTERS.
sections.passageHtml: Note: This part tests your vocabulary depth. You must find a single word that fits grammatically and semantically in all three gaps. Read the contexts carefully to identify collocations and polysemy.

questions.instruction:
38. Find the common word for these three sentences:
- When Erica was trying to complete an important course assignment, she often lost all ........ of time, and sometimes forgot to eat.
- There is no ........ in cleaning the house before the party, but it will certainly need doing afterwards.
- Just entering that ancient building gave Simon a real ........ of how different life must have been in the past.
acceptedAnswers: paraphrased clue for answer sense in the passage.
questions.explanation: Điền 'sense'. Các collocations rất quen thuộc: - 'lose all sense of time' (mất hết khái niệm về thời gian). - 'there is no sense in doing something' (chẳng có ích gì/nghĩa lý gì khi làm cái gì). - 'give someone a real sense of...' (mang lại cho ai cảm nhận thực sự về cái gì). Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 22 Method: complete the gap with "sense" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.

questions.explanation: Điền 'move'. questions.instruction:

- Harry has already spent two ........ at the college.

sections.title: Part 9: Key Word Transformation
sections.instructions: For questions 43–50, complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and six words, including the word given. Write only the missing words in CAPITAL LETTERS.
sections.passageHtml:
Example:

0    Fernanda refused to wear her sister’s old dress.

NOT

Fernanda said that ................................... her sister’s old dress.

The gap can be filled with the words ‘she would not wear’, so you write:

SHE WOULD NOT WEAR

questionGroups.instruction: Complete the second sentence using the key word.

questions.instruction: 43. There has been a sharp rise in the price of petrol this month. RISEN The price of petrol ................................... this month.
acceptedAnswers: paraphrased clue for answer has risen sharply in the passage.
questions.explanation: Điền 'has risen sharply'. - Cấu trúc: Chuyển đổi từ cấu trúc danh từ 'a sharp rise' (sự gia tăng nhanh chóng) sang cụm động từ 'rise sharply' chia ở thì hiện tại hoàn thành 'has risen sharply' để khớp với ngữ cảnh thời gian 'this month'. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 24 Method: complete the gap with "has risen sharply" only after checking the words before and after it. Trap: keep the sentence grammatical and avoid answers that fit the topic but break the structure.

questions.instruction: 44. Lisa was just about to leave the house when the phone rang. POINT Lisa was ................................... the house when the phone rang.
acceptedAnswers: paraphrased clue for answer on the point of leaving in the passage.
questions.explanation: Điền 'on the point of leaving'. - Cấu trúc: Cụm từ 'be on the point of doing something' đồng nghĩa với cụm 'be just about to do something' (sắp sửa, chuẩn bị làm gì đó ngay lập tức). Sau giới từ 'of' ta phải dùng động từ dạng V-ing 'leaving'. Source: CAE 3, Test 1, Page 24 Method: complete the gap with "on the point of leaving" only after checking the words before and after it.
